rows.v: The world's most popular sport — two teams of eleven trying to kick a ball into the opposing goal.
Score more goals than the opponent within 90 minutes by getting the ball into the net, using anything but your hands (except the goalkeeper).
Endurance, footwork, teamwork, and tactical awareness over a large field for a full 90 minutes.
Universal — the World Cup is the most-watched sporting event on Earth; huge in Europe, South America, Africa, and Asia.
A fast, high-scoring team game of shooting a ball through an elevated hoop.
Score more points than the other team by shooting the ball through the hoop — worth 2 or 3 points, or 1 from the free-throw line.
Speed, agility, vertical leap, ball-handling, and constant quick decision-making.
The NBA is a global powerhouse; enormous in the US, and growing fast worldwide.
A hard-hitting team sport of advancing an oval ball down the field in set plays.
Score by carrying or catching the ball into the end zone (touchdown) or kicking it through the posts; advance 10 yards in four 'downs' to keep possession.
Power, speed, strategy, and specialized roles; bursts of intense effort between set plays.
Dominates US sports culture; the Super Bowl is the biggest single-day sporting event in America.
A bat-and-ball game of pitching, hitting, and running around four bases.
Score more runs by hitting the pitched ball and running around all four bases before the fielding team gets you out.
Hand-eye coordination, throwing accuracy, explosive bursts, and strategic patience.
America's 'national pastime'; hugely popular in Japan, South Korea, and Latin America.
A bat-and-ball game between two teams of eleven, played over hours or even days.
Score more runs than the opponent by hitting the bowled ball and running between wickets, while bowling them out.
Technique, concentration, stamina, and tactics sustained over very long matches.
The second most-followed sport globally; a passion in India, Pakistan, Australia, England, and the West Indies.
A physical team sport of carrying, passing, and kicking an oval ball to ground it over the line.
Score by touching the ball down over the opponent's line (a try) or kicking it through the posts; the ball can only be passed backward.
Strength, stamina, toughness, and teamwork, with continuous full-contact play.
Big in New Zealand, South Africa, the UK, France, and Australia; the Rugby World Cup draws global crowds.
A team sport of hitting a ball over a high net so it lands in the opponent's court.
Win points by grounding the ball on the opponent's side; a team has three touches to send it back over the net.
Jumping, quick reflexes, precise hitting and setting, and tight teamwork.
Popular worldwide, including a thriving beach-volleyball version; strong in Brazil, the US, Italy, and Asia.
A team sport of moving a small hard ball with curved sticks into the goal.
Score more goals than the opponent by hitting or pushing the ball into the net with a stick.
Stick skills, speed, stamina, and coordination, often played bent low to the ground.
Strong in India, Pakistan, the Netherlands, Australia, and across Europe; a long-standing Olympic sport.
A racket sport played one-on-one or in pairs across a net on a marked court.
Win points by hitting the ball so the opponent can't return it in bounds; points build into games, sets, and the match.
Power, agility, precision, and mental stamina over long, momentum-swinging matches.
A global sport with four prestigious Grand Slams (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open).
A fast racket sport using a feathered shuttlecock instead of a ball.
Win points by landing the shuttlecock in the opponent's court; it must not touch the ground on your side.
Lightning reflexes, agility, and explosive movement — the shuttle can fly off the racket at over 400 km/h.
Enormously popular in Asia, especially China, Indonesia, India, and Malaysia.
A lightning-fast racket sport played across a small table with a light hollow ball — also called ping-pong.
Win points by hitting the ball so it bounces on the opponent's half and they fail to return it legally.
Extraordinary reflexes, fine touch, and spin control in a tiny, high-speed space.
Dominated by China; played casually and competitively all over the world.
An indoor racket sport played by hitting a small rubber ball against a front wall in an enclosed court.
Win points by hitting the ball off the front wall so the opponent can't return it before it bounces twice.
Explosive fitness, agility, and tactical court positioning in a fast, enclosed space.
Popular in the UK, Egypt (a perennial powerhouse), and across the Commonwealth.
A fast-growing paddle sport blending tennis, badminton, and table tennis on a small court.
Win points by hitting a perforated plastic ball over the net so the opponent can't return it legally.
Quick reflexes and placement over raw power; easy to learn, which fuels its explosive growth.
The fastest-growing sport in the United States, spreading rapidly to all ages.
A combat sport of two fighters throwing punches within a roped ring.
Win by knockout, by stopping the opponent, or on points scored for clean, effective punches over the rounds.
Power, speed, footwork, defense, and conditioning, plus tactical ring craft.
A historic global sport with legendary champions; huge pay-per-view title fights.
One of the oldest sports — two competitors grappling to pin or control each other.
Win by pinning the opponent's shoulders to the mat, or on points for takedowns, escapes, and control.
Strength, leverage, balance, and technique in close, physical grappling.
An ancient Olympic sport; strong traditions in the US, Iran, Russia, Japan, and Eastern Europe.
A full-contact combat sport combining striking and grappling from many martial arts.
Win by knockout, submission (forcing a tap-out), or judges' decision over the rounds.
A complete fighter's skill set — striking, wrestling, and ground grappling — plus elite conditioning.
Driven globally by the UFC; one of the fastest-growing sports of the 21st century.
A Japanese martial art and Olympic sport of throwing and grappling an opponent to the ground.
Win by a clean throw (ippon), or by a hold-down, choke, or arm-lock that forces a submission.
Balance, timing, and technique to use the opponent's force against them, rather than brute strength.
Originated in Japan; a global Olympic sport with strong followings across Europe and Asia.
A striking martial art of punches, kicks, and blocks, practiced for sport, self-defense, and discipline.
In sport karate, score points for controlled strikes to target areas, or perform set forms (kata) judged on precision.
Speed, precision, control, and discipline, with years of practice on form and technique.
Rooted in Okinawa, Japan; practiced worldwide and featured in the Olympics.
A strength sport of lifting a loaded barbell overhead in two precise lifts.
Lift the heaviest possible weight in the 'snatch' and the 'clean and jerk'; the best of each are combined for your total.
Explosive power, technique, mobility, and total-body strength in a single all-out effort.
A classic Olympic sport with powerhouse nations including China, Iran, and the former Soviet states.
Explosive short-distance running races — the purest test of human speed.
First across the finish line wins; races like the 100m and 200m are decided in seconds (or hundredths of a second).
Raw explosive power, fast-twitch speed, and a lightning reaction to the starting gun.
The blue-riband event of every Olympics and World Championships; the 100m crowns the 'fastest human alive.'
Endurance races over middle and long distances on the track, from 800m up to 10,000m.
First to finish wins; success blends raw speed with the stamina to sustain a punishing pace over many laps.
Aerobic endurance, pacing, and a strong finishing kick.
Dominated by East African nations, especially Kenya and Ethiopia; a centerpiece of athletics.
A grueling 42.195 km endurance road race — the ultimate test of distance running.
First across the line over the full 42.195 km wins; for most runners, simply finishing is the goal.
Extraordinary aerobic endurance, mental toughness, pacing, and fuel management over two-plus hours.
Major city marathons (Boston, London, Berlin, New York, Tokyo) draw tens of thousands of runners.
A field event of leaping over a horizontal bar set as high as possible.
Clear the bar at the greatest height without knocking it off; the highest successful jump wins.
Explosive leg power, speed on the approach, and the precise 'Fosbury flop' technique to arch over the bar.
A staple field event at every athletics championship and the Olympics.
A field event of sprinting and leaping for maximum horizontal distance into a sand pit.
Jump the farthest from the take-off board into the pit; the longest legal jump wins.
Sprint speed, explosive take-off, and in-air technique to maximize distance.
A classic track-and-field event since the ancient Olympics.
A spectacular field event of using a flexible pole to launch the body over a high bar.
Clear the bar at the greatest height; vaulters use the pole's stored energy to fling themselves upward.
Sprint speed, gymnastic strength, and fearless precise technique while inverted several meters up.
One of the most thrilling athletics events; vaulters clear over 6 meters at the elite level.
The throwing events of athletics — hurling a heavy object as far as possible (shot put, discus, javelin, hammer).
Throw the implement the greatest legal distance from within the throwing circle or runway.
Raw power, explosive technique, and precise timing to transfer the body's force into the throw.
Core field events at every athletics championship, each with its own specialist technique.
Racing through water using one of four strokes over set distances.
First to touch the wall wins; races span freestyle, backstroke, breaststroke, and butterfly over various distances.
Full-body power, technique, breath control, and endurance with minimal drag.
A marquee Olympic sport; the pool produces some of the Games' biggest stars.
Performing acrobatic dives from a springboard or high platform, judged on form.
Judges score each dive on take-off, technique, the somersaults and twists, and the splash-free entry.
Body control, courage, spatial awareness, and precision in mid-air during a dive lasting under two seconds.
An elegant Olympic sport, with China a long-dominant powerhouse.
Riding ocean waves on a board — part sport, part art, deeply tied to ocean culture.
In competition, judges score riders on the difficulty and style of the maneuvers they perform on each wave.
Balance, wave-reading, paddling fitness, and fearless timing in a constantly changing ocean.
Big in Hawaii, Australia, California, and Brazil; now an Olympic sport.
A physical team sport of throwing a ball into a goal while treading water in a deep pool.
Score more goals than the opponent by throwing the ball into their net, all while staying afloat without touching the bottom.
Tremendous swimming stamina, treading-water leg strength, and physical toughness.
Strong in Hungary, the Balkans, Italy, and along the US coasts; a long-standing Olympic sport.
Propelling a narrow boat through water with oars, racing in crews or solo.
First boat across the line wins; crews of one, two, four, or eight row in perfect unison.
Brutal full-body endurance, leg and core power, and flawless synchronization with the crew.
A prestigious Olympic and collegiate sport; the Oxford–Cambridge Boat Race is a famous tradition.
Racing wind-powered boats around a course, reading wind and water to find the fastest line.
First boat to complete the course wins; success comes from harnessing the wind better than rivals.
Tactics, balance, teamwork, and constant reading of wind, current, and weather.
A global Olympic sport; the America's Cup is its most famous prize.
Racing bicycles, on roads, tracks, or mountains, individually or in teams.
First across the line wins; road racing also rewards team tactics, climbing, and sprinting over long stages.
Leg power, aerobic endurance, tactical racing sense, and bike-handling skill.
The Tour de France is the sport's crown jewel; track and mountain cycling feature at the Olympics.
The pinnacle of motorsport — single-seat open-wheel cars racing at extreme speed.
First across the line over a Grand Prix wins; a season-long points championship crowns the world champion.
Lightning reflexes, physical endurance against G-forces, precision, and split-second decisions at 300+ km/h.
A global spectacle; Grands Prix span Europe, the Middle East, Asia, and the Americas.
Elite motorcycle racing, with riders leaning their bikes to extreme angles at high speed.
First across the line over a race wins; a championship runs across a season of Grands Prix.
Fearless precision, balance, physical strength, and razor-fine throttle and braking control.
Hugely popular across Europe and Asia; the premier class of motorcycle road racing.
Performing tricks and riding on a board with wheels, blending sport and street culture.
In competition, judges score the difficulty, style, and execution of tricks on ramps, rails, and street courses.
Balance, fearlessness, creativity, and endless practice to land technical tricks.
Rooted in California street culture; now a global Olympic sport.
Racing or performing tricks on small, sturdy bikes, on dirt tracks or in skate parks.
BMX racing: first across a jump-filled dirt track wins. BMX freestyle: judges score tricks on style and difficulty.
Explosive power, balance, bike control, and nerve on jumps and ramps.
Both BMX racing and freestyle are Olympic sports with a strong action-sports following.
A fast, physical team sport played on ice, driving a puck into the goal with sticks.
Score more goals than the opponent by shooting the puck into their net, while skating at high speed.
Skating skill, speed, toughness, stick-handling, and split-second teamwork.
Huge in Canada, the US, Russia, and Northern Europe; the NHL is its top league.
An elegant ice sport combining jumps, spins, and footwork into a judged performance.
Judges score the difficulty and quality of jumps, spins, and choreography set to music.
Balance, artistry, explosive jumping power, and precise technique on thin blades.
A glamorous, popular Winter Olympics sport; strong in Russia, Japan, and the US.
Racing down a snow-covered mountain course on skis at high speed.
Fastest time down the marked course wins, through gates in events like slalom, giant slalom, and downhill.
Nerve, leg strength, balance, and precise turning at speeds that can top 130 km/h.
A flagship Winter Olympics sport; strong in the Alpine nations of Austria, Switzerland, and France.
Riding down slopes or performing tricks on a single board strapped to both feet.
Racing events reward the fastest time; freestyle events are judged on the difficulty and style of tricks in the halfpipe or slopestyle course.
Balance, board control, and fearless aerial skill, especially in freestyle events.
A youthful, culture-driven Winter Olympics sport with a big action-sports following.
Racing on ice skates around an oval track, in long-track or short-track formats.
Fastest around the track wins; long-track races the clock, while short-track is a tactical pack race.
Powerful legs, balance, aerobic endurance, and (in short-track) fearless tactics in tight packs.
Popular in the Netherlands (a long-track powerhouse), South Korea, and across Northern Europe.
A strategic ice sport of sliding heavy stones toward a target, sweeping the ice to guide them.
Score points by landing your stones closer to the target's center than the opponent's, over several ends.
Precision, strategy, and teamwork — nicknamed 'chess on ice' for its tactical depth.
Strong in Canada, Scotland, and Scandinavia; a quirky Winter Olympics favorite.
A precision sport of hitting a ball into a series of holes in as few strokes as possible.
The lowest total number of strokes over 18 holes wins; every shot from tee to green counts.
Precision, consistency, course management, and mental composure over a four-hour round.
A global sport with four prestigious majors; huge in the US, UK, and increasingly across Asia.
A precision sport of shooting arrows at a distant target with a bow.
Score the most points by landing arrows closest to the bullseye of the target.
Steady nerves, focus, fine motor control, and consistency under pressure.
A long-standing Olympic sport; South Korea is a dominant powerhouse.
Rolling a heavy ball down a lane to knock over ten pins.
Score points by knocking down as many pins as possible per frame; strikes and spares earn bonus pins.
Consistency, accuracy, spin control, and concentration over ten frames.
A hugely popular recreational sport, especially in the US, with a competitive professional tour.
A precision sport of throwing small darts at a numbered circular board.
Race from 501 down to exactly zero, finishing on a double; precise scoring and checkout strategy decide it.
Steady aim, fine control, mental arithmetic, and nerves of steel under crowd pressure.
A beloved pub sport turned major TV spectacle, especially in the UK and the Netherlands.
Cue sports played on a cloth table, potting balls into pockets (pool) or scoring by caroms (billiards).
In pool, pot your assigned balls and then the black; in carom billiards, score by striking balls in sequence.
Precision, geometry, cue control, and strategic position play for the next shot.
Played in bars and halls worldwide; snooker is a major TV sport in the UK and Asia.
